Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually try to fit in studying whenever I could between work and other responsibilities, which meant it wasn't really organized at all. I'm someone who learns best by doing things hands-on, so I'd often print materials or write things out rather than just reading on screen. With only about seven hours a week to dedicate to studying, I had to be strategic about what I focused on, even if that meant skipping some classes.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I couldn't stick to regular times because my life outside university is unpredictable - I have to work to support myself and that takes priority sometimes. When deadlines approached, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks I might barely touch anything. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I had to manage things.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline coming up or getting a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed. I wasn't the type to browse around the LMS casually - I went in with a specific purpose, grabbed what I needed, and logged out. With limited internet sometimes, I tried to be efficient about it.
Q4: I wouldn't say the LMS ever became a natural routine for me. It felt more like a tool I used when necessary rather than something integrated into my daily life. Maybe early in the semester I tried to check it more regularly, but that faded quickly. The mindfulness exercises they introduced helped me manage my stress a bit, but they didn't really change how often I used the platform itself.
Q5: The downloadable materials were honestly the most useful for me. Once I could download a PDF or document, I could work with it offline, print it out, and actually engage with it physically. I learn better when I can highlight things, write notes in margins, and move around while studying. The videos were okay too, but I'd watch them once and then rely on my own notes afterward.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums. With only two posts the whole course, you can tell it wasn't my thing. I didn't feel like I had time to read through everyone's comments, and honestly, typing out my thoughts online doesn't help me learn the way actually doing something does. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because I had to prioritize what would directly help with assignments.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grades or to understanding something I'd need for the exam. Practical exercises or anything I could apply immediately made sense to me. If something felt abstract or disconnected from what I was being assessed on, I'd probably skip it. Time is precious when you're juggling everything I am.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones got done because they had to - that's where my assignment completion rate comes from. But optional stuff? I'd look at it and think, 'nice, but I don't have the energy or time for this.' There was always something more pressing, and my motivation wasn't high enough to push through for extras.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download everything I could and then work from printed copies or handwritten notes. I'd spread materials out on my floor and physically organize them, walk around while reading aloud, or use flashcards I made myself. That kinesthetic approach is how I actually absorb information, but none of that shows up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many times when I'd spend hours reviewing my printed notes, practicing problems on paper, or even explaining concepts out loud to myself while pacing around my room. I'd be genuinely engaged and learning, but since I wasn't logged into the LMS, there'd be zero evidence of it. My exam score reflects that I was actually studying, even if my online activity doesn't.
Q11: All the time. I'd log in, download what I needed, and then that was it - I wouldn't go back to the LMS for that material again. I'd study from the downloaded files for days or weeks. It was more practical for me since my internet access isn't always reliable, and I preferred having everything saved locally where I could access it anytime without worrying about connectivity.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when stress peaked and my motivation was already low. I felt overwhelmed by trying to balance work, the six online courses, and just life in general. My resilience isn't great, so when things piled up, I'd kind of shut down for a bit. The mindfulness intervention helped me breathe through some of it, but it didn't solve the underlying issues.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and engage with course content. I'd sometimes just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS often got pushed to the bottom of my priority list until deadlines forced my hand.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have a video playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about bills or just feeling too tired to focus. I'd technically be 'watching' but not actually learning anything. Those moments made me feel guilty, but I was too drained to do better in that moment.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they felt low-stakes compared to assignments. I also avoided some readings that seemed dense or confusing - the anxiety of not understanding would make me put them off. I knew they were important, but my brain would just resist engaging with things that felt overwhelming. It's a pattern I recognize but struggle to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator for logging in. Without them, I probably would have engaged even less with the LMS. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active - downloading materials, watching videos quickly, completing assignments. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was what got me to actually do things.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect me, but not always in a motivating way. Good feedback was encouraging, but critical feedback sometimes made me more anxious and less likely to engage right away. I'd need time to process it before I could use it constructively. The feedback loop felt slow sometimes, which made it hard to adjust my approach mid-course.
Q18: The structure was okay, but with six online courses running simultaneously, everything blurred together. Having clear modules helped me know what to prioritize, but the volume of content across all my courses was overwhelming. I appreciated when things were laid out simply, but I often felt like I was just trying to survive rather than thrive within the structure.
Q19: Having downloadable resources made engagement easier - that was crucial for my learning style. But the heavy reliance on discussion forums and online interaction made things harder for me. I'm not someone who engages well through typing; I need to do things physically. Also, when navigation was confusing or things were buried in subfolders, I'd give up looking.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for answers or trying to understand what's expected without actually producing work. Or maybe they're anxious and keep checking things repeatedly without making progress. It could also be that they're engaging with content that doesn't translate into submitted assignments - like watching videos to understand concepts they never get tested on.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was low, but I still got a Merit because I was studying effectively offline. The system can't see me pacing around with flashcards or rewriting notes by hand. Some of us just learn better outside the digital environment, and our grades reflect the work we're doing even if the platform doesn't capture it.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when people log in, what they click on, and how long they spend on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement - whether someone's actually thinking or just scrolling. They completely miss offline study, emotional state, and whether the learning is actually sinking in. Numbers don't capture understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material and trying to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's when I care about what I'm learning and can connect it to something meaningful. For me, it's physical too - writing, moving, doing. Engagement isn't just time spent; it's mental and emotional investment in actually learning something.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much I studied offline with printed materials and handwritten notes. It wouldn't capture my stress levels, my financial worries affecting my focus, or the effort it took just to keep going when my motivation was low. It also can't show that the mindfulness exercises actually helped me cope, even if they didn't change my login frequency. My real engagement happened mostly outside that system.
